item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This get my upvote from me because I just realized the monkey doesn't have eyes.

+1

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The image that was used in this article has been replaced with an image that's cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and is sourced as follows:

Title: SCP-039
Author(s): Charles J Sharp, Morbid Memories
Release year: 2018
Note: Created by Morbid Memories as an edited version of the image below

Source: Wikimedia Commons
License: CC BY-SA 4.0
Title: Proboscis monkey (Nasalis larvatus) male Labuk Bay 2.jpg
Author: Charles J Sharp

Special thanks to Morbid Memories for giving us their blessing in allowing us to use the image. Their DeviantArt can be found here, and their website here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Probusicis monkeys always get my upvote.

For real now, I liked this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Twenty (20) instances of SCP-039 had been apparently living in and maintaining the facility for an unknown period of time." and "as of the time of this writing, five (5) instances of SCP-039 have been born.".
Then why are there only 23? "SCP-039 consists of twenty-three (23) instances of Nasalis larvatus"

I assume that two died between containment and the time of the article's writing, although, yes, some mention of that would be cool.

Favourite part of the article was the last addendum. Seems to imply that the 039s have something to do with why the site is currently abandoned. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't know why, but this tickled me. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Twenty (20) instances of SCP-039 had been apparently living in and maintaining the facility for an unknown period of time." and "as of the time of this writing, five (5) instances of SCP-039 have been born.".
Then why are there only 23? "SCP-039 consists of twenty-three (23) instances of Nasalis larvatus"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Considering how they still have working reproductive organs, it would be interesting to have a line in there where they are introduced to normal proboscis monkeys, even if the resulting offspring [DATA EXPUNGED]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
faceless monkey, cmon man, now i gotta do it,

upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This made me laugh harder than I did reading some of the -Js (and I read through all of them this week). Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is based on a group of NPC's from a Foundation RP, so they started out life working at a Foundation site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like that these are just weird, just and odd thing that sort of has some fringe logic to it but still you don't know why or how they function. Also I like to think that somewhere these monkeys are working in a facility an doing some good work for the Foundation. I know they don't generally use scips for things but still, I like it..

Also the fact that multiple instances tend to distract each other, haha..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's Far Recon, not far reach.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it! +1 for restoring my faith in the wiki after the 1276 incident (check Deletions if u don't know what I mean).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Makes you wonder: are scientists testing such horrible experiments (aside from virus and bacteria) on innocent animals for the "sake" of humanity?

Anyway, very good article. upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really did try to separate it enough from FR to let it stand on its own, but I had a feeling there'd be a member or two who didn't like the attempt. Glad it grew on you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now that I've had some time separate from GMing FR, this SCP has actually grown on me quite a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very much upvoted. What I like the most about this article is the underlying implication that the procedures developed and tested on these monkeys have been introduced with human subjects in mind… I can easily imagine certain real-world manufacturing companies being quite happy to, ahem, condition their workers thus in order to increase their capability of cranking out shiny toys.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I credit Sorts with that idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seemed a bit strange at first, but upvoted.

Wernher was looking at them as they worked today, and noticed some of them clutching their abdomens. Seems that they experience phantom hunger pains, even if they no longer require food. Unless this starts interfering with productivity, we're going to continue as-is.

This… this made me sad. Maybe I'm reading too much into it, but the part about the phantom pains being present, even though the monkeys no longer require food, stood out to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know anything about Far Reach, but I think this is well written and weird enough to be upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"We managed to get it up to about 90% of material tolerances without visible deformation, start at 100 PSI and increase to 300 over 10 seconds. This eliminates the premature stress shearing we were seeing.
This is going to be so awesome!"

I said that last Tuesday. Granted, I was rigging a tear-away bus roof for a cheesy monster movie not uplifting slender-monkeys, but I think the sentiment is similar. The "voice" of the note-taker seems perfectly realistic, to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for MONKEH

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This… seems to be going in five different directions at once. She is a blind, dead, disabled monkey and I don't know if I want her.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I will never see the error page for google (with the trained monkeys) the same way again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My mood doesn't dejenerate so quickly that in one sentence I will spout science shit and the next sentence will be 'DUDE BRO MAN BRO DUUUUDE'. I can understand a progressive exciting yourself. This is is like a mood whiplash hard enough to snap necks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My theory is that they were trying to write Hamlet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, if only for the moment I had where I didn't see what was wrong with the picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, come on, don't you ever get really excited about something and start speaking in simpler tones? Of course his tone's going to degrade a bit, the "writer" (meaning whoever was writing these notes down) was excited and threw professionalism out the window.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know you don't intend it to be a GOI, but the throwaway GOI sold it for me because I've seen something similar in other SCP's and I had an OH, NEAT moment.

super soldiers is headcanoned

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It isn't suppose to be subtle if he's writing down exactly what's happening. Do you want it to be scientific and intelligent or not? :U

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Autopsies have also revealed the digestive system to be absent, and how SCP-039 obtain nutrition, or survive without, is still a subject of research.

This sentence is SU-PA- awkward.

. Seems that they experience phantom hunger pains, even if they no longer require food.

About as subtle as a brick to the face and far less impactful.

After the success of the eyeball thing

You know, the guy seemed quite intelligent while writing notes… But then 'the eyeball thing' ruined it.

Man, I feel like dancing on air

See above. I mean, the previous sentences involve 'With the elimination of sight and the need for food, and with the amplification of hearing and touch, we've managed a total boost of 211% over our initial test subjects'. What kind of guy says that and then begins the next sentence with 'man'?

No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What's this? Continuity?

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, getting a lot of flack from the FR members.

This isn't an attempt at just doing an idea because I'm out of 'em. I just thought they were an idea that could actually be turned into an SCP. I mean, I think Roget and I did a fairly good job at turning them into a serious concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not going to vote at the moment because this just seems to exist to be an inside joke.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's well-written, but… Really? Just … snatch the joke faceless monkeys from Far Recon and write them up? Really? Do we have, like, an idea famine or something? I mean, I vaguely enjoyed the article, but it just seems kind of a waste. But maybe I'm biased, so I'll hold off on voting for now and maybe I'll like it more later, maybe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These are FR proboscis monkeys.
Sorry but I can't take it seriously.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kinda crazy. I upvoted after the addenda. I can't really put my finger on this one way or the other, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The writing quality itself is there. Conceptually, I feel like this lacks some sort of unifying vision.

There's ingredients of overdone concepts sloshing around in here: faceless things (I get the feeling the nose thing was just to deflect accusations of Slendermonkey), sapient animals, Area 51-esque shenanigans, etc. None of it is really coming together for me.

Overall, this feels exactly like something goofy born out of chat, which can work, I suppose, but often doesn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aw, I'd thought the development we had done was enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To clarify: Tox wrote some post about "faceless monkeys" working in the background on site, at which point I decided to fuck with him and have them be literal proboscis monkeys lacking any feature on their aside from a nose.

As for the SCP itself, very novel for a goofy RP, doesn't translate that well to SCP form without any kind of further development.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I assume the facial features probably followed after success of the removal of the digestive system, and at that point they were just like "fuck it let's see what removing other shit does". As for engineering, I imagine they wanted to see if they were intelligent enough to do something complex.

I have no valid argument for the specific use of proboscis monkeys, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It really doesn't do anything for me. The addendums got my hopes up, but then they didn't seem to go anywhere. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get what making facial-feature-less proboscis monkeys has to do with advancing human intelligence. Or at least, I suppose I can see how they'd try it out on monkeys first, but why proboscis monkeys, why remove the facial features, why make them engineers?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A collab between Roget and I.

The idea was inspired by an idea made by Salmander (which was itself inspired by Tox) for Far Recon, so credit to the two of them.

By the way, check out my mad shooping skills.
